text: Leviathans. Sons of the Thousand-Mouthed Levianoch, the sea’s ever-hungry beasts…

runs.text: Leviathans. Sons of the Thousand-Mouthed Levianoch, the sea’s ever-hungry beasts…

text: Caleb turned the page. Mythology didn’t interest him at the moment. The existence of leviathans was a fact, corroborated by countless sailors’ accounts, and in the North an Einfjallan sailor had hunted one down fifty years ago; its skull had since been kept at the local Museum of Natural History. Caleb had seen one alive once; it was magnificent, and he could easily imagine that a single bite would be enough to punch a hole in a ship. What concerned him was whether there were any records of these creatures’ habits.

runs.text: Caleb turned the page.

Mythology didn’t interest him at the moment. The existence of leviathans was a fact, corroborated by countless sailors’ accounts, and in the North an Einfjallan sailor had hunted one down fifty years ago; its skull had since been kept at the local Museum of Natural History. Caleb had seen one alive once; it was magnificent, and he could easily imagine that a single bite would be enough to punch a hole in a ship. What concerned him was whether there were any records of these creatures’ habits.

text: Exceptionally rare. Undeniably predators, and by the reports, territorial.

runs.text: Exceptionally rare. Undeniably predators, and by the reports, territorial.

text: Caleb sighed, then looked at his list and drew a question mark next to the word “leviathan.” It was possible, yet he still thought this wasn’t what he was looking for—unless many leviathans had banded together to hunt ships in cycles at every point of the known world. Besides, every book so far had said that though these creatures were enormous and dangerous, in the end they were still only animals.

runs.text: Caleb sighed, then looked at his list and drew a question mark next to the word “leviathan.” It was possible, yet he still thought this wasn’t what he was looking for—unless many leviathans had banded together to hunt ships in cycles at every point of the known world. Besides, every book so far had said that though these creatures were enormous and dangerous, in the end they were still only animals.

text: He stood from the desk and, one last time, looked over the huge calendar spread on the floor, where the disappearances of ships over the past five years were marked precisely. The where didn’t matter; by their correspondents’ accounts the situation was exactly the same everywhere. In the weeks before every third new moon, disappearances spiked, then in the one or two weeks after new moon there was only the occasional lost ship. Right up until this past year, when the familiar pattern broke and the dots on the calendar evened out instead of waxing and thinning in cycles.

runs.text: He stood from the desk and, one last time, looked over the huge calendar spread on the floor, where the disappearances of ships over the past five years were marked precisely. The where didn’t matter; by their correspondents’ accounts the situation was exactly the same everywhere. In the weeks before every third new moon, disappearances spiked,
then

in the one or two weeks after new moon there was only the occasional lost ship. Right up until this past year, when the familiar pattern broke and the dots on the calendar evened out instead of waxing and thinning in cycles.

text: Two years ago Lord Ebbing, head of the Oceanographic Institute, had convinced the trading companies, and even Parliament, to launch ships only directly after every third new moon. Those who took his advice crossed safely from the Colonies to Rivenstorm, but whatever was behind the cyclical disappearances adapted to the new order. That frightened not only Lord Ebbing but the trading companies as well.

runs.text: Two years ago Lord Ebbing, head of the Oceanographic Institute, had convinced the trading companies, and even Parliament, to launch ships only directly after every third new moon

Those who took his advice crossed safely from the Colonies to Rivenstorm, but whatever was behind the cyclical disappearances adapted to the new order. That frightened not only Lord Ebbing but the trading companies as well.

text: He should have been working on the speech. Caleb was Lord Ebbing’s assistant, and more and more his right hand as the scientist grew older. The rest of the Institute’s staff mostly kept to their own research, and they traveled constantly to observe marine weather and wildlife, and once they had the right instruments, to study chemical composition as well. A few braver scholars even tried to map the oceans’ geography, the undersea ridges and trenches, even though it was life-threatening work.

to study chemical composition as well. A few braver scholars even tried to map the oceans’ geography, the undersea ridges and trenches, even though it was
life-
threatening work.

text: Caleb, however, had stayed at the Academy beside Lord Ebbing. Everything about the seas interested him, so in recent years he hadn’t been able to choose among the many possible but very narrow fields of study, and he knew that whatever he chose would inevitably mean he’d have to ship out. Not that he didn’t want to. In truth he wanted nothing more than to cut through the waves on a fast ship, but his parents had always feared the water and had made him promise, again and again, that he would not go to sea. He suspected that was because his father had nearly been lost to the ocean in a shipwreck once. Even so, his heart ached for it, and deep down he felt that one day he would break that promise. But not today. It comforted him that he was doing important work here too, work that might save lives with Lord Ebbing, and that his life’s work would be more than adding another stack of dust-gathering books to the Academy’s great library.

He suspected that was because his father had nearly been lost to the ocean in a shipwreck once. Even so, his heart ached for it, and deep down he felt that one day he would break that promise. But
not today.
It comforted him

that he was doing important work here too, work that might save lives with Lord Ebbing, and that his life’s work would be more than adding another stack of dust-gathering books to the Academy’s great library.

text: He carefully folded the huge calendar and slipped it into an oversized briefcase. Lord Ebbing’s hearing had been set for the next day, and Caleb had to appear as well. He had to present the situation and why they hadn’t been able to do anything about it. Of course they had discussed many times what he would say, but he always felt it wasn’t enough. If he’d been on the other side—if it were his ships vanishing and his employees lost to the sea—he would have wanted to know why.

runs.text: He carefully folded the huge calendar and slipped it into an oversized briefcase. Lord Ebbing’s hearing had been set for the next day, and Caleb had to appear as well. He had to present the situation and why they hadn’t been able to do anything about it. Of course they had discussed many times what he would say, but he always felt it wasn’t enough. If he’d been on the other side—if it were his ships vanishing and his employees lost to the sea—he would have wanted to know why.

text: Although it was already very late and he should have gone home long ago, he opened the window instead to let in some fresh air. The autumn air was sharp; mist sifted outside, and through it the city’s still-burning lamps flickered like ghostly will-o’-the-wisps. Music still played from a nearby dance hall, but only ragged scraps of melody drifted into the building of the Rivenstorm Scientific Academy. Caleb didn’t know whether anyone besides the night cleaner and the porter was as foolish as he was, working at such an hour.

runs.text: Although it was already very late and he should have gone home long ago, he opened the window instead to let in some fresh air

. The autumn air was sharp; mist sifted outside, and through it the city’s still-burning lamps flickered like ghostly will-o’-the-wisps. Music still played from a nearby dance hall, but only ragged scraps of melody drifted into the building of the Rivenstorm Scientific Academy.
Caleb didn’t know whether anyone besides the night cleaner and the porter was as foolish as he was, working at such an hour.

text: He pulled the Encyclopedia of Marine Megafauna back in front of him and, after the leviathans, turned to the krakens. Everyone knew that the cephalopods were surprisingly intelligent animals.

runs.text: He
pulled the Encyclopedia of Marine Megafauna back in front of him and, after the leviathans, turned to the krakens. Everyone knew that the cephalopods were surprisingly intelligent animals.

text: He rubbed his face. His eyes burned from all the letters, but he couldn’t rest yet. There was still so much to do. Who would it hurt if he set his forehead on the desk for a moment?

runs.text: He rubbed his face. His eyes burned from all the letters, but he couldn’t rest yet. There was still so much to do. Who would it hurt if he set his forehead on the desk for a moment?

text: When the cuckoo clock sounded, Caleb jerked awake, nearly sweeping the pile of papers off the desk where sleep had caught him. Almost without thinking he grabbed for the stack of notes so they wouldn’t scatter across the floor and ruin even the minimal order he had managed to create.

runs.text: When the cuckoo clock sounded, Caleb jerked awake, nearly sweeping the pile of papers off the desk where sleep had caught him. Almost without thinking he grabbed for the stack of notes so they wouldn’t scatter across the floor and ruin even the minimal order he had managed to create.

text: He glanced at the clock, then hurriedly scooped up the papers and the little leather-bound notebook to toss that into his briefcase as well. It was already eight in the morning, and the Western Shipping Company had set Lord Ebbing’s hearing for half past eight. He had originally planned to go home early the night before to sleep, then rise with the sun, bathe, and shave, but the research had dragged on. By seven it had become ten, ten became midnight, and then four in the morning, when sleep finally took him at his desk.

text: So instead of a tidy, well-groomed oceanographer, the committee would get an ink-stained, dark-circled, stubbled man who looked more like a feckless student than a serious scholar under Lord Ebbing’s wing. He might at least have changed his shirt, but there was no time for even that. He didn’t even know, why he didn’t keep a change of clothes in the office for just such occasions.

runs.text: So instead of a tidy, well-groomed oceanographer, the committee would get an ink-stained, dark-circled, stubbled man who looked more like a feckless student than a serious scholar under Lord Ebbing’s wing. He might at least have changed his shirt, but there was no time for even that. He didn’t even know
why he didn’t keep a change of clothes in the office for just such occasions.

text: He ran down the white steps worn glass-smooth, nearly bowling over two arguing scholars, then, with a flurry of apologies, he burst out into the foggy street. In Rivenstorm, October mornings were always foggy, casting even daytime into a ghostly mood over the West Coast’s largest port city. Caleb hunched into his brown leather coat and realized he’d left his hat upstairs. He hated hats anyway, and it wouldn’t have helped the overall impression, so he focused on not being late. Or at least, not very.

text: He left the Academy’s domed building, lined on the outside with white columns and by now aged to green. The massive structure had stood in place for two hundred years, unshakable, proclaiming the importance of knowledge and research as the foundation of progress. More than a hundred scholars could call this place a second home; its lecture hall and library rivaled the university’s, and each floor housed those who studied a different branch of the natural sciences.

place a second home; its lecture hall and library rivaled the university’s, and each floor housed those who studied a different branch of the natural sciences.

text: Over time a little district grew up around the Academy to serve the workers’ needs. Restaurants, cafés, and shops opened, and the owners and Academy staff often knew each other not just by face but by name.

runs.text: Over time a little district grew up around the Academy to serve the workers’ needs. Restaurants, cafés, and shops opened, and the owners and Academy staff often knew each other not just by face but by name.

text: Caleb couldn’t stop at the bakery, which tried to lure him in with the smell of fresh bread, coffee, and sweets, though he was hungry. He strode on, past a café where they were just setting out chairs and tables on the street, and then on past the little bookshop.

text: - Running late again, Mr. André? – asked the old woman standing in front of the nearby greengrocer’s.

runs.text: - Running late again, Mr. André? – asked the
nearby
old woman standing in front of the greengrocer’s.

text: - I just always have to be somewhere other than where I am, Mrs. Mellow – Caleb answered, panting, a tired smile on his face. Mrs. Mellow snatched a shiny red apple from the counter, then tossed it toward him.
